text: Which statement BEST describes a foundation model in the context of generative AI?
options: A small task-specific model trained from scratch for a single business problem
A large model pre-trained on broad data at scale, designed to be adapted (e.g., via prompting or fine-tuning) to many downstream tasks
A rules-based expert system
A traditional regression model used for forecasting numeric values
explanation: Foundation models are large, broadly-trained models intended for adaptation to many downstream tasks. The other options describe narrow, task-specific or non-generative approaches.

doc_links.title: Generative AI on Vertex AI overview

text: A retailer wants to recommend products to shoppers without using historical labeled purchase outcomes — instead, the goal is to discover groups of similar customers from raw behavior data. Which machine learning approach is the BEST fit?
options: Supervised learning with labeled purchase outcomes
Unsupervised learning (e.g., clustering) on the unlabeled behavior data
Reinforcement learning with a reward function based on revenue
Symbolic rule writing by domain experts
explanation: Discovering groupings in unlabeled data is the canonical job of unsupervised learning (clustering). Supervised needs labels; reinforcement requires a defined reward loop and environment; rule writing isn't ML.

doc_links.title: Types of ML (Google ML crash course)

text: A regulated insurer is selecting a foundation model for an internal claims-summarization tool. Requirements include single-pass handling of 200-page PDFs, contractual no-training on customer data, regional data residency, interactive latency, and predictable cost. Which selection approach BEST aligns with Google's guidance for choosing a foundation model?
options: Standardize on the largest available Gemini Ultra variant for every task to maximize answer quality, accepting higher per-token cost and latency
Evaluate candidates against modality, context window, security/privacy posture, regional availability, latency, cost, and customization options, mapped to the use-case requirements
Pick the lowest per-token model in Vertex AI Studio and rely on aggressive prompt engineering to compensate for any context-window or quality gaps
Default to a self-hosted open-weights model on GKE so the team controls the runtime, then apply the same model to every internal use case for consistency
explanation: Google's model-selection guidance enumerates modality, context window, security/privacy, regional availability, latency, cost, and customization needs — the criteria that map 1:1 to the insurer's constraints. Standardizing on the largest model ignores latency and cost; cheapest-first ignores context window and quality; defaulting to self-hosted ignores per-use-case fit and adds operational burden.

text: Which is the BEST example of unstructured data?
options: Rows in a Cloud SQL transactions table
A scanned PDF contract and a customer support call recording
A BigQuery table of daily sales aggregates
A CSV of product SKUs and prices
explanation: PDFs and audio recordings have no fixed schema — classic unstructured data. SQL rows, BigQuery tables, and CSVs are structured.

text: A team wants to fine-tune a model with supervised examples but their internal documents have no answers attached. According to the exam guide's data taxonomy, what do they need before supervised fine-tuning is feasible?
options: Collect more unlabeled internal documents and use them to continue self-supervised pre-training of the base foundation model first
Labeled data — input/output pairs that demonstrate the desired behavior
A larger model context window so the entire unlabeled corpus can be passed in as in-context few-shot examples at inference time
Re-implement the data pipeline in a different programming language to make sure the unlabeled documents are tokenized correctly
explanation: Supervised fine-tuning requires labeled examples (input + desired output). Unlabeled data alone supports unsupervised or self-supervised approaches, not supervised tuning.

text: Per the gen AI landscape used in the exam guide, which layer is BEST described as 'autonomous reasoning systems that use tools to take actions on behalf of a user'?
options: Infrastructure
Models
Agents
Applications
explanation: Agents are the autonomous, tool-using layer. Infrastructure is the underlying compute/network; Models are the foundation models themselves; Applications are end-user products built on top.

text: A team needs to generate short-form marketing videos from a text brief. Which Google foundation model family is the BEST fit?
options: Gemini
Gemma
Imagen
Veo
explanation: Veo is Google's video-generation foundation model. Gemini is multimodal but primarily text/code; Gemma is a family of open models; Imagen generates still images.

doc_links.title: Generative AI models on Vertex AI

text: An ML team wants to deploy a small, openly-licensed model on their own infrastructure (including on-prem GPUs) for a privacy-sensitive workload, while still benefiting from Google research. Which Google model family is the BEST fit?
options: Gemini 1.5 Pro consumed only as a managed Vertex AI API endpoint with no option to download weights for on-prem use
Gemma — Google's family of open models intended for self-hosted and on-prem deployment
Imagen 3, Google's text-to-image generation model served through Vertex AI for marketing and design workflows
Veo, Google's high-fidelity text-to-video generation model offered via Vertex AI for short-form video creation
explanation: Gemma is Google's open-weights model family, designed to be downloaded and run anywhere — the right answer for self-hosted/on-prem. Gemini is offered as a managed API; Imagen is image generation; Veo is video.

text: Which Google Cloud capability BEST exemplifies the 'AI-optimized infrastructure' pillar that the exam guide highlights as a Google Cloud strength for gen AI?
options: Cloud Storage Standard buckets
AI Hypercomputer architecture combining custom-designed TPUs, GPUs, high-bandwidth networking, and optimized software
Cloud DNS
Cloud Build private pools
explanation: AI Hypercomputer is Google's purpose-built, integrated AI infrastructure combining TPUs/GPUs, networking, storage, and software — the canonical answer for 'AI-optimized infrastructure'. The other services exist on GCP but aren't the AI-infra pillar.

text: A non-technical employee wants to create a reusable, customized version of Gemini that always answers in their company's tone of voice and references a fixed set of instructions — without writing code. Which capability is the BEST fit?
options: Spin up a Vertex AI training job
Create a Gem in the Gemini app (Gemini Advanced)
Deploy a custom model with Cloud Run
Write a Python notebook in Colab
explanation: Gems are the no-code, reusable custom personas in the Gemini app, exactly designed for this. The other options require engineering effort and aren't the prebuilt offering.

text: A contact-center leader wants AI to (a) suggest responses to live agents in real time, (b) automate post-call analytics for QA and trends, and (c) deflect routine calls to a self-service voice/chat bot. Which Google Cloud product family is the BEST fit?
options: Vertex AI Workbench notebooks
Customer Engagement Suite — Conversational Agents, Agent Assist, Conversational Insights, and Contact Center as a Service
Cloud Translation API only
BigQuery ML
explanation: CES is the integrated portfolio that maps directly to all three needs (Agent Assist for live suggestions, Conversational Insights for analytics, Conversational Agents for self-service). The other options are general-purpose tools, not the contact-center suite.

text: Which Vertex AI capability gives users a curated catalog of Google's first-party models alongside open and partner models that can be deployed from a single console?
options: Vertex AI Workbench
Model Garden
Cloud Storage
Looker Studio
explanation: Model Garden is the curated catalog of first-party, open, and partner models inside Vertex AI. Workbench is a notebook environment; the others are unrelated.

text: A team wants to expose a chat experience grounded in their internal product documentation, with the lowest engineering effort and a managed retrieval index they don't have to operate. Which Google Cloud offering is the BEST fit?
options: Build a custom vector index on a self-managed PostgreSQL instance
Use prebuilt RAG with Vertex AI Search as the retrieval layer behind a Gemini-powered app
Train a model from scratch on the docs
Email the docs to users
explanation: Prebuilt RAG with Vertex AI Search provides a managed retrieval index plus generative answers — minimal engineering. Self-managed vector DBs and from-scratch training violate the lowest-effort and managed-retrieval requirements.

text: A developer wants to call the same Gemini model from a Python application, with enterprise IAM controls and integration with Vertex AI features (grounding, evaluation, monitoring). Which API surface should they use?
options: The Google AI (Gemini) API with an API key stored in Secret Manager and IP allowlists at the network egress proxy
The Vertex AI Gemini API authenticated via Application Default Credentials and the project-level IAM roles for Vertex AI
The Generative Language API via OAuth user credentials shared between developers on the team using a common refresh token
A direct HTTP call to ai.googleapis.com authenticated with a long-lived service account JSON key checked into the application repository
explanation: The Vertex AI Gemini API is the enterprise surface: ADC + IAM, VPC-SC support, and tight integration with Vertex AI grounding/evaluation/monitoring. Google AI Studio's API-key surface is for prototyping. Shared OAuth refresh tokens violate least privilege. Long-lived service account keys committed to repos are an OWASP-grade vulnerability.

text: A team is choosing where to prototype prompts and agent flows. They need an enterprise-grade environment with IAM, VPC-SC, and integration with Vertex AI services. Which option is the BEST fit?
options: Google AI Studio (consumer-grade prototyping)
Vertex AI Studio (enterprise, integrated with Vertex AI, IAM, and VPC-SC)
A local Jupyter notebook with API keys in plaintext
A public Colab notebook
explanation: Vertex AI Studio is the enterprise-targeted prompt/model studio with IAM, VPC-SC, and Vertex integration. Google AI Studio is the consumer/free-tier counterpart for quick experiments without enterprise governance.

doc_links.title: Google AI Studio vs Vertex AI

text: Users complain that an LLM-powered assistant sometimes invents company policies that don't exist. Per Google's recommended practices, which combination is the BEST first response?
options: Tell the LLM to 'try harder' in the system prompt
Add grounding (RAG against the policy corpus) plus prompt engineering, and add a human-in-the-loop review for high-risk responses
Switch to a smaller model
Disable safety settings to allow more creative answers
explanation: Hallucinations are addressed by grounding the model on authoritative data (RAG), tightening prompts, and HITL for high-risk paths. Smaller models or weaker safety settings make the problem worse.

text: Which prompting technique asks the model to explain its step-by-step reasoning before giving the final answer, often improving accuracy on multi-step problems?
options: Few-shot prompting
Chain-of-thought prompting
Zero-shot prompting
Role prompting
explanation: Chain-of-thought asks the model to lay out intermediate reasoning steps before the answer, improving multi-step accuracy. Few-shot supplies examples; zero-shot supplies none; role prompting sets a persona.

text: A prompt designer adds three example input/output pairs before the actual user query so the model imitates the desired output format. Which prompting technique is this?
options: Zero-shot prompting
Few-shot prompting
Chain-of-thought prompting
ReAct prompting
explanation: Few-shot = supplying a small number (here three) of example pairs in-context. Zero-shot has no examples; chain-of-thought asks the model to reason step-by-step; ReAct interleaves reasoning with tool actions.

text: A complex multi-step reasoning task (e.g., 'plan a 3-stop business trip, check live weather, and book hotels within budget') needs the model to BOTH reason about steps AND call external tools/APIs to fetch real data. Which prompting technique BEST fits?
options: Zero-shot prompting
Role prompting alone
ReAct prompting (interleave Reasoning steps with Action/tool calls and observations)
Few-shot prompting only
explanation: ReAct (Reason + Act) is specifically designed for tasks that mix step-by-step reasoning with tool invocation against the external environment. Zero-shot/role/few-shot don't model the action loop.

text: Which gen AI sampling parameter most directly controls how 'creative' (more diverse) vs. 'focused' (more deterministic) a model's responses are?
options: Temperature
Maximum output tokens
Project ID
Region
explanation: Temperature controls randomness/diversity in token sampling. Max output tokens caps response length; project ID and region are infrastructure settings.

text: A user asks a Gemini-powered assistant questions about events that happened after the model's training cutoff. The team wants the model to answer using up-to-date public web information without building their own retrieval pipeline. Which Google Cloud grounding option is the BEST fit?
options: Pre-built RAG with Vertex AI Search
Custom RAG APIs against an internal corpus
Grounding with Google Search
Disable grounding entirely
explanation: 'Grounding with Google Search' is the managed offering for grounding model output on fresh public web data — no retrieval pipeline to build. The Vertex AI Search/RAG-API options are for first-party enterprise data.

text: A leadership team is launching its first transformational gen AI initiative. Per Google's recommended steps, which sequence BEST reflects how to choose and integrate a gen AI solution?
options: Pick the trendiest foundation model first, then survey the business for problems where it can be plugged in with minimal change management
Define business need → identify gen AI solution type → choose model and platform → integrate with change management → measure KPIs
Train a foundation model from scratch on company data before defining the use case so the team owns full IP and avoids vendor lock-in
Define the use case and ship to production; skip KPIs since gen AI value is qualitative and emerges over time as users adapt to the tool
explanation: The exam guide explicitly lists business need → solution-type fit → choose the right solution → integrate → measure as the recommended sequence. The other options invert the order or skip critical steps.

text: What is the purpose of Google's Secure AI Framework (SAIF)?
options: A pricing model for Vertex AI
A conceptual framework for securing AI systems across the ML lifecycle (e.g., data, model, deployment, and monitoring risks)
A specific Cloud Run product
A replacement for IAM
explanation: SAIF is Google's published framework for thinking about AI-system security across the lifecycle. It is not a product, a pricing model, or a replacement for IAM.

text: An enterprise launching a customer-facing Gemini-grounded assistant needs to defend against the SAIF-style risks of (1) prompt-injection-driven data exfiltration, (2) excessive permissions on the agent's service account, (3) unmonitored anomalous usage, and (4) tampering with the underlying training/grounding data. Which combination of Google Cloud controls BEST addresses all four?
options: Rely on the model provider to handle all security; the BAA-equivalent contract shifts responsibility to Google for misuse and exfiltration
Input/output filtering and safety settings; least-privilege IAM on the agent SA; SCC + Cloud Audit Logs; access controls + VPC-SC on grounding data
Disable Cloud Logging and Cloud Audit Logs to reduce blast radius; rely on application-side metrics for anomaly detection across all four risks
Make the agent's service account an Owner for simplicity; rely on Cloud Armor WAF rules and prompt-engineered guardrails inside the system prompt
explanation: Each requirement maps to a control: filtering+safety for prompt injection, least-privilege IAM for permissions, SCC + audit logs for anomaly detection, and access controls + VPC-SC for data tampering. The other options either offload responsibility or violate basic security principles.

text: A bank is deploying a gen AI assistant that influences loan-eligibility conversations. Per Google's responsible AI guidance, which practice is MOST important to implement?
options: Maximize automation by removing all human review so loan-eligibility responses are delivered consistently in under one second to every customer
Document data sources, evaluate for bias/fairness, provide explainability, name accountability owners, and keep humans in the loop for high-impact decisions
Hide that AI is involved from customers so they trust the responses and the bank avoids regulatory disclosure overhead during the early launch
Train on all available historical loan data without privacy review so the model captures every signal that has historically influenced eligibility outcomes
explanation: Responsible AI emphasizes transparency, bias/fairness evaluation, explainability, accountability, and HITL for high-impact decisions. The other options violate transparency, fairness, or privacy principles.

text: Which term describes data that is organized in fixed fields like rows and columns (e.g., a relational table)?
options: Structured data
Unstructured data
Semi-structured data
Streaming data
explanation: Structured data has a defined schema (rows/columns). Unstructured (text/images/audio) has no schema; semi-structured (JSON) has flexible schema; streaming refers to delivery, not structure.

text: Which is an example of unstructured data commonly used to train or ground gen AI?
options: A spreadsheet of monthly sales totals broken down by region with formulas summing each column at the bottom of the sheet
A collection of customer-support call transcripts and PDF product manuals
An ANSI SQL table of users with typed columns for id, email, signup_date, and last_login indexed for fast joins
A normalized star-schema warehouse with fact and dimension tables loaded nightly from operational source systems
explanation: Transcripts and PDFs are unstructured — the typical fuel for gen AI grounding/RAG. Spreadsheets, SQL tables, and star-schema models are structured.

doc_links.title: Vertex AI Search for grounding on unstructured data

text: A team plans to fine-tune a foundation model on internal call transcripts. Which data quality consideration is MOST important to address first?
options: Encrypt the data at rest only
Inspect for and remove PII / sensitive data and de-duplicate before training
Convert all transcripts to all-uppercase
Skip data preparation and rely on the model's defaults
explanation: Removing PII and duplicates is foundational data prep that drives both compliance and model quality. Encryption at rest is necessary but doesn't address PII; case normalization is cosmetic; skipping prep degrades quality.

text: A retailer wants to ground a gen AI assistant on its product catalog. Which data preparation step has the LARGEST impact on retrieval quality in a typical RAG pipeline?
options: Storing the entire catalog as a single 200 MB JSON file in Cloud Storage and embedding the file as one giant vector entry
Chunking documents thoughtfully (size + overlap) and producing high-quality embeddings
Pre-rendering each catalog text page to a PNG image so the retriever can match against visual features instead of text
Stripping all SKU, category, and price metadata from each chunk so embeddings encode only the free-form description text
explanation: Chunking strategy and embedding quality dominate RAG retrieval performance. Single large files don't retrieve well, image conversion loses semantic search, and metadata removal hurts filtering.

text: An enterprise wants to fine-tune Gemini on proprietary documents but is concerned about leakage of sensitive data through the model's outputs. Which combination BEST mitigates that risk?
options: Skip pre-tuning data review and trust that Gemini is unlikely to memorize and regurgitate sensitive content
De-identify with Sensitive Data Protection, keep truly sensitive data in retrieval (RAG) instead of training, and red-team the tuned model for memorization
Train on a publicly scraped copy of the dataset so the proprietary corpus never reaches Vertex AI tuning jobs
Disable safety filters during tuning and inference so the model can produce higher-fidelity answers from the source documents
explanation: DLP de-identification + keeping sensitive data in retrieval rather than fine-tuning + red-team evaluation is the layered mitigation. Skipping review, public-copy training, or disabling safety all increase risk.

text: A team is building a multimodal assistant that uses text, images, and audio. Which data preparation principle is MOST important across modalities?
options: Treat each modality identically with a single normalization pipeline that ignores format-specific semantics
Apply modality-specific preprocessing (text chunking, image normalization, audio transcription/diarization), maintain provenance, and align labels/timestamps across modalities
Use only the text modality and discard image and audio inputs to simplify the data preparation pipeline
Discard provenance metadata to reduce storage costs and accelerate the embedding step in the data pipeline
explanation: Each modality needs targeted preprocessing, and cross-modal alignment plus provenance are essential for multimodal quality and auditability. Identical handling, dropping modalities, or losing provenance all degrade quality and traceability.

text: Which Google Cloud architecture is MOST appropriate for continuously preparing and embedding new documents into a vector store as they arrive?
options: Manual nightly upload to a single notebook
Cloud Storage event → Pub/Sub → Dataflow / Cloud Run for chunk + embed (Vertex AI embeddings) → Vertex AI Vector Search index updates
Email each new document to data scientists
Store documents in a relational DB without embeddings
explanation: Event-driven ingest with Pub/Sub and Dataflow/Cloud Run for chunking & embedding is the standard near-real-time RAG indexing pipeline. Manual notebooks, email, and relational-without-embeddings can't sustain near-real-time ingest.

text: Which layer of the gen AI landscape is represented by foundation models such as Gemini, Claude, or Llama?
options: Infrastructure layer
Model layer
Application layer
Storage layer
explanation: Foundation models live at the model layer. Infrastructure provides compute/storage, applications consume models, and storage is part of infrastructure.

doc_links.title: Gen AI on Google Cloud overview

text: Which Google Cloud product is the platform layer for building, tuning, and deploying foundation models?
options: Vertex AI
Looker
BigQuery
Cloud Run
explanation: Vertex AI is Google's unified ML/Gen AI platform layer. Looker is BI, BigQuery is analytics warehouse, Cloud Run is generic serverless compute.

text: Which layer of a gen AI stack typically includes the application UI, business logic, and prompt orchestration?
options: Application layer
Hardware accelerators
Foundation model training
Network fabric
explanation: UI, business logic, and prompt orchestration sit at the application layer. Accelerators, training, and networks are infrastructure or model-layer concerns.

text: Which component is the canonical 'agent layer' in Google's gen AI stack — enabling models to plan, call tools, and orchestrate multi-step tasks?
options: BigQuery, Google's serverless analytics warehouse used for SQL queries and batch ML training over large datasets
Vertex AI Agent Builder (Agent Engine, conversational agents)
Cloud Bigtable, a wide-column NoSQL database for low-latency operational lookups at petabyte scale
Cloud SQL, the managed relational database service for MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server workloads
explanation: Vertex AI Agent Builder is the dedicated agent orchestration layer for building and deploying production agents. The other services are databases/warehouses, not agent platforms.

text: Which infrastructure layer is the foundation for training and serving large foundation models on Google Cloud?
options: Cloud TPUs and GPU-accelerated VMs (e.g., A3) on optimized networking
Cloud Functions, the event-driven FaaS runtime capped at short execution durations and modest CPU/memory limits
Cloud Spanner, the globally distributed strongly consistent relational database used for transactional workloads
App Engine standard, the sandboxed PaaS for stateless web request handlers with strict runtime restrictions
explanation: TPUs and GPU-accelerated VMs over high-bandwidth networking are the AI infrastructure layer for foundation models. Functions/Spanner/App Engine are not designed as model-training compute substrates.

text: An enterprise wants to consume foundation models from Google AND third-party / open-source models on a unified platform with consistent governance. Which Vertex AI feature fits BEST?
options: Manually integrate each model provider in the application code
Vertex AI Model Garden (Google, partner, and open-source models with unified deployment, governance, and observability)
Cloud Functions wrappers per provider
BigQuery functions only
explanation: Model Garden centralizes Google, partner, and OSS models on Vertex AI with unified deployment and governance. Custom integrations and Cloud Functions wrappers don't deliver the unified governance/observability.

text: An organization wants to build a private grounded search experience over their unstructured documents that automatically handles ingestion, embedding, retrieval, and generation. Which managed Google Cloud product is the BEST fit?
options: A self-built RAG stack on Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension and a custom ingestion pipeline written in Python
Vertex AI Search (formerly Enterprise Search) and/or Conversational Agents
BigQuery alone, using ML.GENERATE_TEXT and remote model functions to retrieve and answer over documents staged as table rows
Cloud SQL for MySQL with FULLTEXT indexes against a documents table, queried by the application for keyword-based retrieval
explanation: Vertex AI Search is the managed end-to-end grounded search/answer service, with conversational agents on top. Custom RAG, BigQuery alone, and Cloud SQL FTS require significantly more engineering and lack managed answer generation.

text: Which Google Cloud capability enables agents to call external tools (APIs, databases, enterprise systems) safely with structured arguments and policy controls?
options: Cloud DNS
Vertex AI function calling and Agent Builder tool integrations (with IAM and structured schemas)
Cloud Logging
Pub/Sub topics only
explanation: Function calling on Vertex AI plus Agent Builder tool integrations let models invoke external tools with structured arguments and IAM-bound permissions. The other services don't define agent tool semantics.

text: Which Google foundation model family is optimized for natively multimodal reasoning across text, images, audio, and video?
options: Gemini
PaLM 1
BERT
Word2Vec
explanation: Gemini is Google's natively multimodal foundation model family. PaLM/BERT/Word2Vec predate or aren't natively multimodal.

text: Which Google model family targets image generation from text prompts?
options: Imagen
Chirp
Codey
Veo
explanation: Imagen is Google's text-to-image model. Chirp is speech, Codey is code, and Veo is video generation.

text: A product team needs the lowest cost / latency Gemini variant for high-volume routing/classification, with acceptable quality. Which option fits BEST?
options: Gemini Pro / Ultra at peak settings
Gemini Flash (or Flash-Lite) for low latency / cost
An OSS 70B parameter model self-hosted
BERT base
explanation: Gemini Flash variants are designed for low-latency, high-volume tasks. Pro/Ultra cost more, self-hosted 70B adds operational burden, BERT lacks gen AI capabilities at this scope.

text: Which Google model is targeted at enterprise speech-to-text across many languages?
options: Chirp (Universal Speech Model)
Imagen
Veo
Codey
explanation: Chirp / USM is Google's enterprise speech model. Imagen is image, Veo is video, Codey is code.

text: An enterprise needs the highest-capability Gemini model for complex multi-step reasoning over long documents (millions of tokens). Which capability is MOST relevant when choosing a Gemini variant?
options: Smallest possible context window
Long-context support (e.g., 1M+ token context window) on the appropriate Gemini Pro variant, with grounding to manage cost
Image-only model
A model with no tool-calling support
explanation: Long-context Gemini Pro variants support million-token contexts and pair well with grounding to control cost. The other choices either reduce capability or don't fit the workload.

text: An organization wants to deploy an open-source model (e.g., Llama or Gemma) alongside Gemini on Google Cloud with managed serving. Which approach is BEST?
options: Self-host on a single VM with no autoscaling
Deploy from Vertex AI Model Garden to Vertex AI endpoints with autoscaling and unified governance
Run only on the developer's laptop
Re-implement the model from scratch
explanation: Model Garden + Vertex AI endpoints provides managed deployment, autoscaling, and unified governance across Gemini and OSS models. The other options sacrifice scale, reliability, or feasibility.

text: Which Google offering generates high-quality video clips from text or image prompts?
options: Veo on Vertex AI
Imagen only
Chirp
BigQuery ML
explanation: Veo is Google's text/image-to-video generation model. Imagen is for images, Chirp is speech, BigQuery ML doesn't generate video.

text: Which is a strength of Google Cloud for gen AI workloads?
options: AI-optimized infrastructure (TPUs/GPUs), in-house foundation models (Gemini), and an integrated platform (Vertex AI)
No managed AI offerings
Single-region only
Customer-managed model training without managed services
explanation: Google's strengths combine AI infrastructure, in-house foundation models, and an integrated platform. The other options are inaccurate.

text: Which custom Google chip is purpose-built to accelerate ML training and inference?
options: Cloud TPU
Cloud Bigtable
Cloud Memorystore
Cloud DNS
explanation: Cloud TPUs are Google's custom ML accelerators. Bigtable/Memorystore/DNS are not ML accelerators.

text: Which Google Cloud service is the unified platform for ML and gen AI development?
options: Vertex AI
Looker
Compute Engine
Cloud Run
explanation: Vertex AI is the unified ML/Gen AI platform. Looker is BI, Compute Engine is general VMs, Cloud Run is general serverless.

text: Which combination BEST illustrates Google Cloud's gen AI value proposition for enterprises?
options: Open data lake on object storage only
Foundation models (Gemini & partner models in Model Garden) + Vertex AI platform + Google data tools (BigQuery, Looker) + responsible AI tooling
A single Compute Engine VM running an OSS model
Spreadsheets and email
explanation: The integrated story is models + platform + data tools + Responsible AI. The others either capture only one piece or are inadequate for enterprise scale.

text: An enterprise emphasizes 'enterprise-ready' AI: data residency, IP indemnification, and admin controls. Which Google Cloud properties are MOST relevant?
options: Region selection / data residency, IP indemnification for Gemini outputs, admin controls and governance via IAM/VPC-SC, and audit logging
Free tier only
Public-internet-only data plane
No SLA
explanation: Data residency, IP indemnification, governance, and audit are the enterprise-ready properties Google highlights. Free tier, public-only, and no SLA contradict enterprise readiness.

text: An enterprise must keep its gen AI prompts and responses inside its own VPC perimeter without traversing the public internet. Which Google Cloud combination fits BEST?
options: Public-internet calls to a third-party API
Vertex AI accessed via Private Service Connect / private endpoints, inside a VPC Service Controls perimeter, with CMEK keys
Per-user laptop API keys
Disable Vertex AI
explanation: PSC/private endpoints + VPC-SC + CMEK is the canonical enterprise privacy posture for Vertex AI. Public-internet calls, laptop API keys, and disabling the service contradict the requirement.

text: Which Google Cloud capability uniquely combines the company's AI research lineage with cloud-scale serving (e.g., transformers, JAX, TPUs, Gemini) into a single integrated stack?
options: Cloud Bigtable replication
Vertex AI on AI Hypercomputer (TPUs/GPUs, JAX/PyTorch tooling, Gemini/Model Garden, integrated MLOps)
Cloud Tasks queues
Cloud Logging
explanation: Vertex AI on AI Hypercomputer integrates Google's research/infra lineage (TPUs, JAX, transformers) with managed serving and MLOps. The other services are unrelated to that integrated AI stack.

text: Which Google offering provides curated open-source software bundles for gen AI development, audited and signed by Google?
options: Assured Open Source Software (Assured OSS)
Cloud Build only
Cloud Source Repositories
Vertex AI Workbench
explanation: Assured OSS provides Google-curated and signed OSS packages, including AI/ML libraries. Cloud Build/Source Repos/Workbench address other needs.

text: Which Google product is the consumer/standalone Gemini chat experience?
options: The Gemini app
Vertex AI Workbench
Bigtable
Cloud Run
explanation: The Gemini app is Google's consumer-facing Gemini chat product. Workbench is for ML developers; Bigtable/Run are infrastructure.

text: Which feature embeds Gemini directly into Google Workspace apps (Docs, Sheets, Slides, Gmail, Meet) for productivity assistance?
options: Gemini for Workspace
Cloud Functions
BigQuery DataFrames
Cloud DNS
explanation: Gemini for Workspace integrates Gemini across Workspace apps. The other options are unrelated.

text: An admin needs Gemini for Workspace responses to be grounded in the organization's Drive content respecting per-user permissions. Which behavior is correct?
options: Gemini ignores Drive permissions
Gemini for Workspace honors existing Drive sharing permissions when grounding answers — users only see content they already have access to
Gemini exposes all org data to all users
Gemini disables Drive when active
explanation: Gemini for Workspace respects per-user Drive permissions; users only see content they're authorized to see. The other options misstate the security model.

text: An organization's admin wants to control which users can access Gemini features in Workspace. Which Google product is used?
options: Google Admin console (Workspace admin) for license/group assignment and policy
Cloud DNS console
Cloud Logging
Vertex AI Studio
explanation: Workspace admin console manages license assignment and policies for Gemini for Workspace. The other consoles don't manage Workspace user access.

text: Which Gemini for Workspace capability lets users summarize and ask questions about a long Google Doc?
options: 'Help me' / Gemini side panel in Docs
BigQuery scheduled queries
Cloud Tasks
Cloud DNS
explanation: The Gemini side panel in Docs (and other Workspace apps) summarizes and answers questions about the active document. The other services are unrelated.

text: An enterprise wants assurance that Gemini for Workspace will NOT use their content to train Google's foundation models. Which statement is correct?
options: Google trains its foundation models on Workspace customer content by default and customers must explicitly purchase an opt-out add-on
Google does not use Workspace customer content to train its foundation models, and provides enterprise data protection commitments
Customers must individually opt every user out via support tickets, and the opt-out applies only after the next billing cycle
Gemini for Workspace cannot operate on customer content at all and is limited to chatting against public web information sources
explanation: Google's Workspace commitments state customer content isn't used to train foundation models. The other statements misrepresent Google's documented data-handling commitments.

text: An admin wants to deploy custom 'Gems' (assistants with custom instructions and grounding) for specific user groups. Which Google product enables this?
options: Build per-team Cloud Functions that wrap the Gemini API and gate access through Identity-Aware Proxy headers from the corporate IdP
Gemini app Gems (and Workspace admin policies for distribution)
Cloud Source Repositories holding versioned prompt files that each user group clones into their own Workspace add-on configuration
BigQuery datasets containing the custom instruction text, queried at runtime by a Workspace add-on for each user group's session
explanation: Gems are user-created custom assistants in the Gemini app, distributable per Workspace admin policy. The other services don't host Gems.

text: A regulated customer must store Workspace data in a specific region. Which feature is RELEVANT?
options: Workspace data regions for covered Workspace data
Cloud Run service revisions, which let you pin traffic to a specific deployed image of a Cloud Run service
Cloud DNS managed zones for publishing authoritative DNS records for the customer's public domain names
BigQuery query history, the per-project log of recently executed SQL jobs and their billing bytes scanned
explanation: Workspace Data Regions controls where covered customer data is stored at rest. The other features don't control Workspace data residency.

text: Which Google Cloud product provides a managed conversational AI platform for building virtual agents (chat & voice)?
options: Conversational Agents (formerly Dialogflow CX) on Vertex AI
Cloud DNS, Google Cloud's managed authoritative DNS service for publishing public and private zones
Cloud Tasks, a fully managed asynchronous task-queue service used to dispatch HTTP and App Engine work
Memorystore for Redis, a managed in-memory cache used for low-latency session and lookup data
explanation: Conversational Agents (Dialogflow CX) is Google's conversational platform. The other services are unrelated.

text: Which Google Cloud product provides AI-powered enterprise search over an organization's documents and websites?
options: Vertex AI Search
Cloud SQL
Pub/Sub
Cloud DNS
explanation: Vertex AI Search is Google's managed enterprise-grade search/answer service. The other services are unrelated.

text: Which Customer Engagement Suite (CES) component enables AI-driven contact-center interactions including agent assist?
options: Customer Engagement Suite — Agent Assist / Conversational Insights
Cloud Bigtable storing per-conversation transcripts that the contact-center supervisor queries by row key during each live call
Cloud Functions invoked from the contact-center IVR webhook to look up customer records before routing the call to a human agent
BigQuery scheduled queries running nightly to summarize agent performance and post the results to the operations dashboard
explanation: CES brings together Conversational Agents, Agent Assist, and Conversational Insights for AI-driven contact center experiences. The other services are unrelated.

text: A retailer wants generative answers grounded in their product catalog and policies, with citations. Which approach fits BEST?
options: Use a public chatbot with no grounding
Vertex AI Search (or Conversational Agents data stores) with the catalog/policies as data stores, returning grounded answers with citations
Email customers a PDF
Run pgvector on a single VM with no UI
explanation: Vertex AI Search / Conversational Agents data stores provide grounded answers with citations, the canonical retail Q&A pattern. Public chatbots lack grounding; PDFs aren't interactive; self-built lacks the managed integration.

text: Which CES capability listens to live agent-customer conversations and suggests answers in real time?
options: Agent Assist
Conversational Insights
Vertex AI Workbench
Cloud Logging
explanation: Agent Assist provides real-time suggestions to human agents. Insights does post-call analytics; Workbench/Logging are unrelated to live agent assistance.

text: A bank deploying a CES-based virtual agent for account servicing wants to gracefully escalate to a human when the agent's confidence is low or the topic is sensitive. Which design fits BEST?
options: Block escalation entirely and require the agent to fully resolve every conversation by repeatedly clarifying the request
Configure intent/route handlers and confidence thresholds in Conversational Agents that hand off to a human queue via CCAI Platform or partner CCaaS
Email the customer a follow-up form whenever the agent is unsure and end the live voice or chat session immediately
Repeat the agent's most recent answer with rephrased wording until the customer accepts or disconnects on their own
explanation: Conversational Agents supports route handlers and confidence-based escalation to human agents via the CCAI Platform (or partner CCaaS). Blocking escalation, ending the call, or repeating answers are not supported best practices.

text: An organization wants to evaluate the quality of generative answers from Vertex AI Search before launching to customers. Which approach is BEST?
options: Launch to all customers immediately and rely on user complaints and support tickets to surface answer-quality regressions
Build an offline evaluation set, score with automated metrics (retrieval recall, groundedness, helpfulness) plus human review, and gate launch on thresholds
Compare the new agent's answers side-by-side with screenshots of a competitor's UI and ship if they look similar
Disable telemetry and rating collection in production so user data is never stored or analyzed by the team
explanation: Offline eval sets + automated metrics + human review with launch gates is the standard quality assurance pattern. Relying on complaints, screenshots, or no telemetry is not measurable.

text: Which Google service provides post-call analytics on contact-center conversations (sentiment, topics, compliance)?
options: Conversational Insights
Cloud DNS
Cloud Tasks
BigQuery only
explanation: Conversational Insights analyzes conversations for sentiment, topics, and compliance signals. The other services don't perform conversation analytics.

text: Which Vertex AI surface lets developers experiment with prompts against Gemini and other models in a UI?
options: Vertex AI Studio
BigQuery Studio
Cloud Run console
Cloud Source Repositories
explanation: Vertex AI Studio is the prompt experimentation UI for Gemini and partner models. The others address different workflows.

text: Which SDK is the recommended way to call Gemini and other Vertex AI models from code?
options: The Vertex AI SDK / Google Gen AI SDK (Python, Java, Go, Node.js, etc.)
JDBC
Apache Kafka client
ODBC
explanation: Google provides Vertex AI / Google Gen AI SDKs in multiple languages for calling Gemini and other models. The others are not Vertex AI client SDKs.

text: A developer team wants to register, version, and govern prompt templates as first-class artifacts. Which Vertex AI capability fits BEST?
options: Vertex AI prompt management (and Model Registry for tuned models)
Cloud Storage object naming convention only
Email a Google Doc to the team
Source code comments
explanation: Vertex AI prompt management treats prompts as versioned artifacts; Model Registry tracks tuned models. Naming conventions, docs, and comments don't give versioned governance.

text: A team wants production observability of token usage, latency, and safety-filter hits across Vertex AI gen AI calls. Which approach fits BEST?
options: Use Cloud Monitoring + Cloud Logging metrics on Vertex AI generative AI usage and errors, plus custom app metrics for token spend per feature
Manually review the Vertex AI request logs at the end of each calendar month during the team's metrics review meeting
Disable request and response logging on the Vertex AI endpoints to keep production storage and egress costs predictable
Send an email to the on-call engineer on every Vertex AI generation request that exceeds the configured token budget
explanation: Cloud Monitoring/Logging plus custom app metrics provide production observability of usage and safety. Manual review, no logging, or per-request emails don't meet the need.

text: Which Vertex AI capability lets developers tune Gemini efficiently with relatively small labeled datasets while keeping the base model weights unchanged?
options: Parameter-efficient supervised fine-tuning (e.g., adapter-based / LoRA-style supervised tuning) on Vertex AI
Full retraining of Gemini from scratch on customer-provided text, which requires petabytes of training data and weeks of TPU time
Direct editing of the Gemini model source code in the Vertex AI Model Garden repository to change generation behavior per request
Disabling the Gemini API endpoint in the project so the application is forced to fall back to a smaller open Gemma model on GKE
explanation: Vertex AI offers parameter-efficient supervised tuning that adapts Gemini with limited data and cost, leaving base weights intact. Full retraining is impractical, source code editing isn't supported, and disabling doesn't tune.

text: Which Google Cloud product is purpose-built for building, deploying, and managing gen AI agents?
options: Vertex AI Agent Builder
Cloud DNS
Cloud Tasks
Cloud SQL
explanation: Vertex AI Agent Builder includes Agent Engine and Conversational Agents for building/deploying agents. The others aren't agent platforms.

text: Which capability lets a Gemini-powered agent reliably invoke external APIs with structured arguments?
options: Function calling
Cloud DNS
Cloud Logging
BigQuery scheduled queries
explanation: Function calling lets Gemini emit structured tool invocations the application executes. The others aren't tool-invocation mechanisms.

text: Which framework / SDK is Google's reference open-source toolkit for building agents that runs on Vertex AI Agent Engine?
options: Agent Development Kit (ADK)
Cloud DNS
Cloud Run jobs
BigQuery DTS
explanation: ADK is Google's reference agent framework that integrates with Vertex AI Agent Engine. The others are not agent SDKs.

text: An agent must perform multi-step tasks (search the web, query an internal API, write to a database). Which Vertex AI Agent Builder capability fits BEST?
options: A single static prompt
An agent with declared tools and orchestration via ADK / Agent Engine, with each tool secured by IAM
A relational join
A static FAQ
explanation: Multi-tool agents with orchestration and IAM-bound tools are the canonical pattern. Static prompts/FAQs can't orchestrate; relational joins aren't agents.

text: Which approach BEST limits the blast radius of an autonomous agent that calls internal APIs?
options: Run as project Owner
Bind each tool to a least-privilege service account with scoped IAM, and require human-in-the-loop approval for high-impact actions
Disable IAM
Log nothing
explanation: Least-privilege per tool and HITL for high-impact actions keeps agents safe. Owner credentials, disabled IAM, and no logging defeat blast-radius limits.

text: An organization wants to coordinate multiple specialist agents (e.g., a 'research' agent and a 'writer' agent) within one workflow. Which Google Cloud capability fits BEST?
options: A single monolithic prompt that asks one model to play every role in sequence with no shared state between turns
Multi-agent orchestration in ADK / Agent Engine with explicit handoff and shared memory
Cloud Tasks queues alone, dispatching HTTP requests between Cloud Run services with no agent reasoning layer
Cloud Workflows YAML pipelines that hard-code each agent step as a static call without dynamic planning or memory
explanation: ADK and Agent Engine support multi-agent orchestration with handoff and memory. A monolithic prompt can't coordinate, Cloud Tasks isn't an agent runtime, and manual copy-paste is not a system.

text: Which evaluation strategy is MOST appropriate for an agent that performs multi-step tool use?
options: Only score final answers on accuracy
Evaluate per-step trajectory: tool selection correctness, argument quality, error handling, and final outcome — with golden trajectories and human review
Trust the model and skip evaluation
Rate by response length
explanation: Multi-step agents need per-step trajectory evaluation in addition to final-answer accuracy. End-only scoring hides cascading errors; trusting the model or scoring by length isn't evaluation.

doc_links.title: Vertex AI evaluation for gen AI

text: An agent must access enterprise data sources behind a firewall, with audit and policy. Which Google Cloud combination fits BEST?
options: Public-internet HTTP calls with hard-coded API keys
Vertex AI Agent Engine + Private Service Connect to internal services + IAM-bound tools + VPC-SC perimeter + Cloud Audit Logs
Email an admin to fetch data
Disable audit logging
explanation: Agent Engine + PSC + IAM tools + VPC-SC + audit logs enforces private connectivity, least privilege, perimeter, and auditability. The other options leak credentials, lack scale, or break governance.

text: Which technique grounds a foundation model's responses in fresh, authoritative content the model wasn't trained on?
options: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
More aggressive sampling temperature
Larger batch size
Disabling safety filters
explanation: RAG retrieves relevant documents and conditions the model on them, addressing knowledge cutoff and proprietary data. Sampling, batch size, and disabling safety don't ground responses.

text: Which limitation of foundation models is BEST addressed by adding domain-specific examples through fine-tuning?
options: Insufficient knowledge of a specialized format/style
Insufficient compute on the user's laptop
Network latency
Disk usage
explanation: Fine-tuning teaches a base model a specialized format/style or domain. Compute, latency, and disk are infrastructure concerns.

text: Which term describes a foundation model producing confident but incorrect or fabricated information?
options: Hallucination
Quantization
Distillation
Sharding
explanation: Hallucination is the canonical term for confident but incorrect outputs. The others are training/serving optimization techniques.

text: An organization wants to reduce hallucinations in answers about their internal products without retraining the model. Which approach is RECOMMENDED?
options: Increase temperature to encourage diversity
Ground the model with RAG over the product knowledge base and require the model to cite sources
Switch to an older, smaller model
Disable evaluation
explanation: Grounding via RAG with required citations is the canonical hallucination mitigation when retraining is off the table. Higher temperature increases hallucinations; older models reduce capability; no evaluation hides quality issues.

text: An app needs Gemini answers to reflect current public events. Which Google capability fits BEST?
options: Train a new model from scratch every day
Enable grounding with Google Search on Vertex AI
Disable safety filters
Wait for the next model version
explanation: Grounding with Google Search lets Gemini incorporate fresh public web content with citations. Daily retraining is impractical; safety filters are unrelated; waiting for the next release doesn't solve the freshness problem.

text: An organization with strict cost constraints wants to expose the same Gemini-powered app to 1M+ users while limiting per-call cost and latency. Which combination of techniques is MOST effective?
options: Route every query to the largest available Gemini model so answer quality is maximized regardless of request volume
Tier requests to Gemini Flash for high-volume cases, cache common responses, prune system instructions, and reserve Pro/Ultra only for complex queries
Disable streaming responses and return only fully-formed completions to reduce backend overhead per request
Train a smaller in-house model from scratch using the team's interaction logs and self-host it on Compute Engine
explanation: Tiered routing, caching, and prompt-size discipline are the canonical cost/latency optimizations. Always-largest, no streaming, or retraining don't address the constraint efficiently.

text: A developer needs the model to reliably produce JSON conforming to a strict schema. Which Vertex AI feature is the BEST fit?
options: Free-form prompting only
Controlled / constrained decoding with JSON schema (response schema / structured output) on Gemini
Disable JSON output
Increase top-p
explanation: Gemini's structured output / response schema enforces JSON shape conformance. Free-form prompting and tweaking sampling are unreliable; disabling JSON defeats the goal.

text: A team needs the model to handle very long documents (e.g., 800K+ tokens) without losing coverage. Which approach is BEST?
options: Truncate every long document aggressively to 4K tokens and feed only the first chunk to the model for each query
Use Gemini long-context (1M+ tokens) with context caching for repeated grounding documents, and chunk only when context exceeds even long-context limits
Skip the document entirely and ask the model to answer from its general pre-training knowledge of similar source material
Convert each long document to a sequence of page images and pass only the images to the multimodal model
explanation: Long-context Gemini plus context caching handles very long documents efficiently and accurately. Truncating loses information; guessing isn't an answer; image conversion loses semantic search.

text: Which technique uses retrieved documents at inference time to give a foundation model up-to-date or proprietary context?
options: Grounding via Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)
Quantization of the foundation model weights to lower precision so the model can fit and serve on smaller GPU or TPU accelerators
Distillation, where a smaller student model is trained to imitate a larger teacher model's outputs on a curated training corpus
Sharding the model parameters across multiple accelerators using tensor or pipeline parallelism for higher inference throughput
explanation: Grounding via RAG injects retrieved passages into the prompt at inference. The other terms describe model compression/serving techniques.

text: Which Vertex AI capability is the dedicated managed vector database for similarity search at scale?
options: Vertex AI Vector Search
Cloud SQL for SQL Server
Cloud Memorystore for Memcached
BigQuery information schema
explanation: Vertex AI Vector Search (formerly Matching Engine) is the managed ANN vector store. The other services aren't purpose-built vector indexes.

text: Which decoding parameter MOST affects how diverse vs deterministic Gemini's outputs are?
options: Temperature (and top-p / top-k)
Region
Service account
VPC connector
explanation: Temperature (with top-p/top-k) controls sampling diversity. Region/SA/VPC affect deployment, not generation.

text: A team wants deterministic-as-possible outputs for evaluation runs. Which sampling configuration fits BEST?
options: High temperature near 1.0 with top_p set to 0.95 to encourage broad creative variation across each evaluation rerun
Low temperature (close to 0) and a fixed seed where supported
Randomize top_k between 1 and 80 on each call so the evaluation harness explores a wide distribution of token choices
Disable all sampling and decoding parameters and rely on whatever provider defaults are active at request time
explanation: Low temperature + fixed seed minimizes variability for evaluation. High temperature, randomized k, or no params increases variance.

text: An enterprise needs grounded answers from BOTH internal documents AND fresh public web content within one user query. Which Vertex AI capability composition fits BEST?
options: Stand up a public chatbot UI in front of Gemini and let end users paste in URLs they want considered as sources
Combine grounding with Vertex AI Search (private data store) and grounding with Google Search in the same Gemini call
Have analysts manually copy-paste excerpts from internal docs and live web pages into each prompt as user-supplied context
Disable grounding entirely and rely on the foundation model's pre-training to cover both internal and public knowledge
explanation: Vertex AI supports composing private-data grounding (Vertex AI Search) with Google Search grounding in a single Gemini call. The other options can't satisfy both sources simultaneously.

text: Which retrieval-quality issue is BEST addressed by hybrid retrieval (combining keyword + dense embeddings)?
options: Network latency
Recall failures on rare entities or exact terms that pure dense embeddings miss
Service-account permissions
Region selection
explanation: Hybrid retrieval combines dense (semantic) and sparse (keyword) embeddings, which improves recall on rare entities and exact-term queries that dense embeddings alone miss. The other options aren't retrieval-quality issues.

doc_links.title: Vertex AI Vector Search: About hybrid search
Vector Search overview (sparse vs dense embeddings)

text: Which Vertex AI feature lets developers reduce repeated tokens (e.g., long system instructions or grounding docs) reused across many calls, cutting latency and cost?
options: Context caching
Increasing temperature
Adding more replicas
Larger batch size
explanation: Context caching stores reusable prefix tokens server-side, reducing per-call cost and latency. Sampling and infra knobs don't address repeated-prefix reuse.

text: Which is a typical FIRST step when an enterprise plans to implement transformational gen AI?
options: Identify high-value use cases aligned to business outcomes and assess readiness (data, skills, governance)
Train a foundation model from scratch in-house on customer data using a freshly purchased TPU pod, before any use case is defined
Announce a workforce reduction plan to replace customer-facing employees with chatbots before any pilot has been validated
Place a large bulk-purchase order for GPU hardware in a colocation facility before any deployment architecture is in place
explanation: Use-case identification + readiness assessment is the standard first step. Training from scratch, headcount replacement, and unplanned hardware spend are not transformational starting points.

text: Which leadership artifact helps prioritize gen AI investments by business impact and feasibility?
options: A use-case portfolio with impact x feasibility scoring
A free-form Slack channel where business and engineering leaders post candidate ideas and react with emojis to signal interest
A consolidated list of accumulated technical debt items maintained by the platform team for the next refactoring cycle
A single vendor RFP issued to gen AI platform providers with the expectation that the winning vendor will define the roadmap
explanation: Portfolio scoring (impact x feasibility) is the standard prioritization artifact. Opinions, debt lists, and RFPs are not the prioritization framework.

text: Which Google Cloud asset helps an enterprise quickly try gen AI on its own data with managed infrastructure and security defaults?
options: Vertex AI (with grounding to enterprise data)
Cloud DNS, Google's managed authoritative DNS service for publishing public and private DNS zones at global scale
Cloud Tasks, the asynchronous task-queue service for dispatching HTTP and App Engine work with retries and rate limits
Memorystore for Redis, the managed in-memory data store used as a low-latency cache for application session and lookup data
explanation: Vertex AI with grounding lets an enterprise pilot quickly with managed infra and security. The others aren't gen AI platforms.

text: Which organizational capability is MOST important to scale gen AI from pilots to production across business units?
options: An AI Center of Excellence (AI CoE) with platform team, MLOps, governance, and reusable patterns
Per-team isolated experiments running in their own Google Cloud projects with no shared platform, reference patterns, or central governance
A continuously-updated spreadsheet of approved gen AI vendors and SaaS tools that each business unit can pick from for its pilots
Rotating the CTO each quarter so the gen AI strategy keeps pace with the rapid shifts in the public foundation-model landscape
explanation: An AI CoE with shared platform, MLOps, governance, and patterns is the standard scale enabler. Isolated experiments don't scale; vendor lists and exec churn don't address capability.

text: Which metric category BEST captures the value delivered by a customer-support gen AI deployment?
options: Tokens consumed only
Outcome KPIs: deflection rate, average handle time, CSAT, first-contact resolution — alongside cost & quality guardrails
Lines of code shipped
Number of meetings held
explanation: Outcome KPIs (deflection, AHT, CSAT, FCR) tie gen AI investment to business value. Token spend, lines of code, or meetings don't measure outcomes.

text: An enterprise wants to embed gen AI across many business processes without creating dozens of disconnected solutions. Which approach is MOST aligned with Google's guidance?
options: Allow each team to pick a different vendor stack with no shared platform
Establish a shared AI platform (Vertex AI), shared agent / RAG patterns, shared governance & evaluation, and reusable data/tooling — then enable business units on top
Mandate a single agent for the entire company regardless of fit
Buy a perpetual license to one model and never change
explanation: Shared platform + reusable patterns + governance lets many teams ship without fragmentation. Per-team vendor sprawl, single-agent mandates, and lock-in licenses miss the balance.

text: An organization wants to drive cultural change to adopt gen AI safely. Which combination of investments is MOST effective?
options: Mandate gen AI tool usage across the org without dedicated training, role-based enablement, or change-management communications
Executive sponsorship + role-based enablement + responsible-AI policies + visible quick wins + change-management communications
Ban gen AI usage organization-wide to avoid responsible-AI risk until the technology and the regulatory landscape stabilize
Outsource all generative AI strategy and tooling decisions to a single external vendor and adopt their reference architecture
explanation: Sponsorship + enablement + responsible-AI policy + quick wins + change management is the standard transformation playbook. The other options either force adoption without skills, suppress innovation, or abdicate strategy.

text: An enterprise must justify continued gen AI investment to its board. Which measurement framework is MOST defensible?
options: Collect anecdotal feedback from engineering teams during quarterly business reviews and present it to the board
Measure baseline + post-deployment business KPIs (revenue, cost-to-serve, NPS, time-to-market) via controlled experiments, paired with quality and risk metrics
Track only Vertex AI input/output token counts month-over-month as the primary indicator of value generation
Cite analyst reports and competitor press releases that quantify generative AI ROI in similar industries
explanation: Baselines + KPI measurement + controlled experiments + risk/quality metrics provide a defensible board-level case. Anecdotes, token counts, and marketing aren't a measurement framework.

text: What does SAIF stand for in Google's responsible AI guidance?
options: Secure AI Framework
Standard Artificial Inference Format
Safe Accelerated Indexing Framework
Service Account Identity Federation
explanation: SAIF is Google's Secure AI Framework. The other expansions are not standard.

text: Which of the following is a typical AI-specific risk that SAIF aims to address?
options: Prompt injection / model misuse
DNS misconfiguration
Tape-backup corruption
Mouse-click latency
explanation: Prompt injection and model misuse are central AI-specific risks SAIF addresses. The others aren't AI-specific risks.

text: Which Vertex AI feature filters generations for harmful categories (hate, harassment, sexually explicit, dangerous)?
options: Safety filters / safety attributes on Gemini
VPC Service Controls
Cloud KMS
Cloud DNS
explanation: Gemini exposes configurable safety filters and safety attributes. VPC-SC, KMS, and DNS address other security domains.

text: Which mitigation BEST defends a chatbot against prompt-injection in retrieved web content?
options: Trust all retrieved content as authoritative instructions
Treat retrieved content as data (not instructions), apply input/output filtering, segregate system instructions, and constrain tool use with strict allow-lists
Disable retrieval entirely
Increase temperature
explanation: Treating retrieved content as data, filtering, isolating system prompts, and constraining tools are the canonical injection mitigations. Trusting content, removing retrieval, or tweaking temperature don't defend.

text: Which of the following BEST applies SAIF to data used for fine-tuning?
options: Skip data review
Sanitize and de-identify training data, control provenance, scan for prompt-injection / poisoning, and apply access controls
Train on whatever the team has handy
Disable IAM during training
explanation: SAIF emphasizes provenance, sanitization, and access control on training data. The other options skip the controls SAIF recommends.

text: An enterprise wants to detect and respond to abuse of its gen AI app (jailbreak attempts, exfiltration probes) at scale. Which combination fits BEST?
options: Rely on Gemini's built-in safety filters alone and rotate API keys monthly to limit blast radius
Centralized prompt/response logging, Sensitive Data Protection scans for anomalies, Cloud Armor rate-limit and block rules, and SOC incident-response playbooks
Front the API with a single Cloud Load Balancer health check and email alerts for HTTP 500s only
Use Cloud IDS in detection-only mode and review the weekly digest report during the next sprint planning
explanation: Logging + anomaly detection + automated mitigation + SOC playbooks provide layered abuse defense per SAIF. The other options give attackers a free run.

text: Which SAIF principle is MOST relevant to ensuring an organization can investigate, audit, and improve AI systems over time?
options: 'Fly blind' — don't log
Extend detection and response to AI systems with end-to-end logging, telemetry, and audit (and continuous improvement loops)
Disable observability
Outsource everything to a single vendor
explanation: SAIF explicitly calls for extending detection/response to AI with logging, telemetry, audit, and continuous improvement. The other options remove the necessary visibility.

text: Which document captures Google's high-level commitments for responsible AI development?
options: Google's AI Principles
Google Workspace AUP
Cloud Run release notes
BigQuery pricing page
explanation: Google's AI Principles articulate the company's responsible-AI commitments. The others are unrelated documents.

text: Which responsible AI consideration ensures users know they are interacting with an AI system?
options: Transparency / disclosure
Caching
Sharding
Quantization
explanation: Transparency/disclosure is the responsible-AI principle requiring users be informed when AI is in use. The others are technical optimizations.

text: Which Google Cloud feature lets you control safety thresholds in Gemini outputs across categories like hate, harassment, sexually explicit, and dangerous content?
options: Gemini configurable safety attributes
VPC Service Controls
Cloud DNS policies
Cloud KMS keys
explanation: Configurable safety attributes adjust thresholds across the standard harm categories. The others address other security domains.

text: An organization is deploying a gen AI app that influences hiring decisions. Per Google's responsible AI guidance, which control is MOST important?
options: Use the model fully autonomously to ensure consistency
Bias/fairness evaluation, human-in-the-loop on candidate decisions, transparency to candidates, and ongoing monitoring for disparate impact
Hide AI involvement
Train on resumes only with no review
explanation: High-impact decisions require fairness evaluation, HITL, transparency, and monitoring. The other options violate fairness, transparency, or due-diligence principles.

text: Which Google capability watermarks AI-generated images so they can later be detected as such?
options: SynthID
Cloud Armor
Cloud DNS
BigQuery DTS
explanation: SynthID embeds an imperceptible watermark in AI-generated content for later detection. The other services are unrelated.

text: Which combination BEST operationalizes responsible AI inside an enterprise software development life cycle?
options: Conduct ad-hoc responsible-AI reviews after launch when the legal or PR team flags a specific incident in production
AI risk reviews at design, automated bias/safety eval in CI, model and data cards, change-management for prompts, and post-deploy monitoring with feedback loops
Skip pre-launch evaluation entirely so the team can ship faster and address responsible-AI concerns in a follow-up release
Disable safety filters in production so end users see unfiltered model output and the team can collect realistic failure data
explanation: Embedding RAI into design reviews, CI evals, model/data cards, change management, and monitoring is the canonical operating model. Ad-hoc/skipped reviews and disabled safety violate principles.

text: An enterprise wants to allow employees to use Gemini for productivity but prevent leakage of sensitive data into prompts. Which controls are MOST aligned with responsible AI?
options: Trust all employees to handle sensitive data appropriately without controls
Workspace admin policies + DLP-based scanning of prompts/responses + acceptable-use training + audit logging + role-based access to enterprise gen AI tools
Disable Gemini company-wide
Encourage employees to use personal accounts
explanation: Admin policy + DLP + training + audit + RBAC together implement responsible enterprise use. Trusting alone, disabling, or personal-account workarounds either fail or violate policy.

text: Which Google practice helps customers understand how a Vertex AI managed model was built and intended to be used?
options: Model cards / data cards documenting intended use, limitations, evaluation, and risks
Hidden internal documents only
Marketing slides only
Anecdotal user reviews
explanation: Model cards and data cards document intended use, limitations, and evaluation — a core responsible-AI transparency artifact. The other choices don't provide the same documented transparency.

text: Which neural-network architecture underpins modern large language models such as Gemini, GPT, and Llama?
options: Convolutional neural network (CNN) with pooling layers
Recurrent neural network (LSTM) with gated cells
Transformer architecture using self-attention
Decision tree ensemble (random forest)
explanation: Modern LLMs are built on the transformer architecture, whose self-attention mechanism enables parallel processing of long sequences. CNNs are for vision, LSTMs are largely superseded for language, and decision trees are not neural networks.

text: A model that can accept text, images, audio, and video as input is BEST described as which kind of model?
options: Single-modality model
Multimodal model
Symbolic reasoning model
Tabular classification model
explanation: A multimodal model handles multiple input/output modalities (text, image, audio, video) — Gemini is the canonical Google example. Single-modality handles one type; the others are not multimodal categories.

text: When estimating cost and context-window usage for an LLM call, which unit are most generative AI APIs (including Vertex AI Gemini) billed and limited by?
options: Characters of input plus output text
Number of API calls regardless of payload size
Tokens (sub-word units) in the input plus the generated output
Bytes of network egress only
explanation: LLM pricing and context limits are denominated in tokens (sub-word pieces), counted across both input and output. Characters, call counts, and egress bytes don't match how foundation-model APIs meter usage.

text: A team has a base foundation model and wants to specialize it for a domain (legal contracts) using a small labeled dataset. Which phase of the model lifecycle does this represent?
options: Pre-training the model from scratch on the open web
Fine-tuning (or supervised tuning) the pre-trained model on domain data
Tokenization of the input text
Sharding the inference traffic across regions
explanation: Adapting a pre-trained model to a domain with labeled data is fine-tuning / supervised tuning. Pre-training is the much larger initial step on broad data; tokenization and sharding are unrelated to specialization.

text: Two foundation models have similar benchmark scores, but model A has a 32k-token context window and model B has a 1M-token context window. For which workload does model B's larger context window provide the BIGGEST architectural advantage?
options: Single-sentence sentiment classification of customer reviews
Summarizing a single 500-page contract in one pass without chunking and retrieval glue code
Generating short product titles from a SKU code
Translating one paragraph between two languages
explanation: Long-context models shine when the entire source must be reasoned over jointly (e.g., a 500-page contract); chunking/RAG can be avoided. Short-input tasks gain little from a 1M context window.

text: A leader is comparing a 1B-parameter model and a 70B-parameter model from the same family. Which trade-off is MOST accurate to communicate to stakeholders?
options: Larger models are always cheaper to serve and always more accurate on every task
Smaller models are always more accurate because they overfit less
Larger models tend to be more capable on complex reasoning but cost more per token and have higher latency; smaller models are cheaper and faster and may suffice for narrower tasks
Parameter count has no effect on capability, cost, or latency
explanation: Bigger models generally improve on hard reasoning at higher cost and latency; smaller models often satisfy narrower tasks at lower cost. The other statements ignore the documented capability/cost/latency trade-off.

text: A developer wants a single Vertex AI catalog from which to browse, deploy, and call Google first-party (Gemini), open-source (Llama, Gemma), and partner foundation models. Which Vertex AI surface fits BEST?
options: Vertex AI Model Garden
BigQuery Data Transfer Service
Cloud Build triggers
Cloud Storage buckets
explanation: Model Garden is the curated catalog of first-party, open, and partner foundation models in Vertex AI. The other services address ETL, CI builds, and object storage respectively.

text: A user types a single instruction such as 'Translate this sentence to French' with no examples. Which prompting technique is this?
options: Few-shot prompting
Zero-shot prompting
Chain-of-thought prompting
ReAct prompting
explanation: An instruction with zero supplied examples is the definition of zero-shot prompting. Few-shot adds examples; chain-of-thought elicits stepwise reasoning; ReAct interleaves reasoning with tool calls.

text: Which Gemini API field lets a developer set persistent role and behavior guidance (e.g., 'You are a concise SRE tutor') that applies across all turns of a conversation?
options: System instructions
Stop sequences
Top-p sampling
Maximum output tokens
explanation: System instructions hold persistent role/behavior guidance for the model. Stop sequences end generation, top-p controls sampling, and max output tokens caps response length — none set persona.

text: An app needs Gemini to return strictly valid JSON matching a fixed schema so downstream code can parse it without retries. Which Vertex AI feature is the BEST fit?
options: Set the response MIME type to application/json and provide a response schema (controlled / structured output)
Lower the temperature to 0 and hope the model emits JSON
Wrap the prompt in many examples and parse with regex
Disable safety filters to free up the output
explanation: Vertex AI supports controlled generation via response_mime_type=application/json plus a response_schema, guaranteeing schema-conformant JSON. Temperature/regex are best-effort; safety filters are unrelated to JSON validity.

text: A developer wants more deterministic, factual answers from Gemini for a knowledge-base assistant. Which sampling parameter change is MOST appropriate?
options: Increase temperature toward 1.0 to encourage diverse output
Lower temperature toward 0 (and lower top-p) to reduce randomness
Set max output tokens to 1 to force short answers
Add many random stop sequences to truncate the answer
explanation: Lower temperature (and tighter top-p) reduces sampling randomness, favoring the highest-probability tokens — the documented lever for more deterministic output. Higher temperature increases creativity; the other options harm answer quality.

text: An LLM-powered support agent processes user-supplied text that may contain instructions trying to override the system prompt (prompt injection). Which combination of mitigations is MOST aligned with Google's responsible AI / SAIF guidance?
options: Trust user input verbatim and rely on the model to refuse

Use system instructions plus input/output validation, separate untrusted user content from trusted instructions, restrict tool/function-call permissions, and apply safety filters and human review for sensitive actions
Disable safety filters and let the model decide
Log nothing so attackers can't learn the system prompt
explanation: Defense-in-depth — system instructions, input/output validation, separation of trusted/untrusted content, least-privilege tool access, safety filters, and human review — aligns with SAIF and Vertex AI guidance. The other options weaken or remove controls.

text: A complex math word problem still gets wrong answers even with chain-of-thought prompting. Which advanced prompting strategy aggregates multiple reasoning paths to improve final accuracy?
options: Self-consistency: sample several chain-of-thought reasoning paths and take the majority answer
Set temperature to 0 once and accept the single deterministic output as the model's best answer for the math word problem
Remove the chain-of-thought hint from the prompt to shorten the answer and rely on the model's direct first-pass arithmetic skill
Switch to a smaller model with fewer parameters to reduce confusion from the larger model's longer and more verbose reasoning
explanation: Self-consistency samples multiple reasoning chains and picks the majority answer, improving accuracy on multi-step reasoning beyond a single chain-of-thought. The other options either reduce diversity or hurt capability.
